sections.Definitions: Weaning = whole process of liberating from support and the tube. Weaning failure = failing an SBT or needing reintubation/NIV within 48h (some 72h). Extubation failure = need for reintubation. Traditional: simple (1st SBT, 30-59%), difficult (2nd-3rd SBT within 7 days, 26-40%), prolonged (>3 SBTs or >7 days, 6-30%). WIND: Group no-weaning (~24%), Group 1 short (<24h), Group 2 (1 day-1 week), Group 3 prolonged (>7 days). WEAN SAFE: 65% weaned by day 90; 22.4% delayed >=5 days (often high sedation); higher sedation and delayed initiation = modifiable factors. Risk factors: age, frailty, immunocompromise, comorbidities (mortality 16% none vs 34% >=3); medical patients >20% extubation failure vs surgical <10%; chronic cardiac failure on admission associated with BETTER outcomes. Extubation failure/reintubation mortality ~30-50% vs 5-10%; WIND mortality 5.8/16.5/29.8% (Groups 1-3).
sections.Weaning Outcome Predictors: RSBI (RR/VT after 2 min) - sensitive screen for candidates who might pass a confirmatory SBT, does NOT confirm SBT success. Diaphragm ultrasound (thickening fraction DTF, excursion) predicts weaning with satisfactory accuracy. Weaning Index (WI) = RSBI x EI x VDI (EI=PIP/MIP, VDI=MV/10); higher predictive ability than single parameters; combining WI with DTF further improves accuracy.
sections.The course of weaning: 4 steps: illness resolves; readiness suspected/assessed (PaO2/FiO2>200 with PEEP<=5, haemodynamic stability, adequate consciousness/cough, RSBI<100); SBT (T-piece most accurately simulates post-extubation; low pressure support up to 7cmH2O decreases WOB and may overestimate readiness; 30 min usually adequate, up to 120 min in high-risk); extubation. Table 2 success: RR<35, HR<140 or HRV>20%, SatO2>90%/PaO2>60 on FiO2<0.4, SBP 80-180 or <20% change, no distress. Table 3 failure - clinical: diaphoresis, nasal flaring, increasing effort, HR rise>40, arrhythmias, hypotension; gas exchange: PetCO2 rise>10, pH<7.32 or drop>0.07, P/F<150, SpO2 fall>5%. Delayed SBT initiation -> longer MV/ICU stay.
sections.Diagnostic approach to weaning failure: Failing an SBT/extubation places a patient in the difficult-to-wean group. Common SBT-failure causes: incomplete resolution of critical illness, errors in readiness assessment, a new problem. Primary mechanisms: respiratory pump insufficiency, cardiovascular dysfunction, neuromuscular disorders, psychological factors, metabolic/endocrine disease - alone or combined.
sections.Respiratory pump insufficiency: Probably most common cause. Table 4 (capability): decreased capacity - decreased centre output, phrenic nerve dysfunction, muscle pump dysfunction (hyperinflation, decreased O2 delivery, acidosis, electrolytes, malnutrition, fatigue, critical illness neuromyopathy, VIDD); increased workload - increased ventilatory requirements (CO2 production, dead space) and mechanical loads (resistance, dynamic elastance, intrinsic PEEP). Table 5 (load): increased resistance - tube (small diameter/sputum/kinking), central/small airways; reduced compliance - chest wall (abdominal pressure, pleural fluid/ascites, pneumothorax, obesity, flail chest, kyphoscoliosis), lung (intrinsic PEEP, alveolar filling/ARDS, atelectasis, interstitial disease/fibrosis, pneumonia). Laryngeal injury -> post-extubation stridor; cuff leak test if high risk. COPD: dynamic-hyperinflation-induced geometrical distortion. Critical illness neuromyopathy: sepsis, COPD, corticosteroids/neuromuscular blockers. TTi rises through SBT in those who fail; >0.15 = fatigue. Prevent atrophy: maintain some inspiratory effort, avoid injurious effort, avoid excessive PEEP, avoid excessive assist/low drive, use proportional modes, avoid myotoxic drugs.
sections.Cardiovascular Dysfunction: First described 1988 (Lemaire), obstructive lung disease; heart failure in up to 42% of unsuccessful SBTs. WiPO in ~12% of SBTs, 36% of weaning failures. Mechanism: decreased intrathoracic pressure on spontaneous breathing increases venous return (RV preload) and LV afterload; increased WOB -> increased muscle activity, decreased intrapleural pressure; RV dilation increases interdependence, decreasing LV compliance/increasing filling pressure; dyspnoea/anxiety/adrenergic tone + impaired gas exchange -> myocardial ischaemia. COPD + LV disease = high risk. LV diastolic dysfunction with preserved EF increasingly recognised. BNP higher in failure. Diagnosis: echocardiography Doppler preferred (historically PAOP); also BNP, EVLW. Management: fluid removal/optimise loading; diuretics/vasodilators/inotropes. SBT: pressure support generally preferred (less stress), but T-piece can unmask WiPO.
sections.Other causes of weaning failure: Brain dysfunction (mainly delirium, CAM-ICU) associated with difficult weaning/failed extubation; anxiety/depression also.
sections.Diagnostic workup and management of wean: Table 6 (respiratory pump): diagnosis - waveform inspection, gas exchange, dynamic compliance/PEEPi/resistance, lung/abdomen ultrasound/CXR, airway/tube patency (bronchoscopy), cuff leak; capability - MIP/Pdimax, ultrasound, phrenic stimulation. Therapy - treat disease, optimise airway (bronchodilation, steroids), tracheotomy (rarely); optimise sedation, mobilisation, minimise asynchrony, nutrition. Table 7 (cardiac): ECG/echo before-during SBT, SvO2, BNP, rule out ischaemia; therapy - optimise fluid (diuretics, restrictive), reduce afterload (dobutamine, levosimendan, nitrates), treat ischaemia. Table 8 (brain/psych): CAM-ICU, screen depression/anxiety; therapy - optimise sedation, mobilisation, dexmedetomidine, sleep. Table 9 (metabolic/endocrine): electrolytes/glucose/hormones; therapy - glucose control, normalise electrolytes, hormone replacement.
sections.The role of NIV and HFNC in weaning fail: 3 timepoints: facilitation - after non-successful SBT, esp COPD PaCO2>45 (reduced invasive MV/ICU stay/pneumonia); prevention - after successful SBT in high-risk (>65 + chronic CV/resp disease), decreases reintubation/ICU mortality, benefit more pronounced in high-risk; alternating NIV+HFNC decreases reintubation vs HFNC alone. Curative/rescue - 2 large RCTs found NO benefit and INCREASED ICU mortality (preserved oxygenation delays reintubation despite high WOB/self-inflicted lung injury); HFNC INFERIOR to conventional O2 in this rescue context; intubate if no improvement 1-2h.
sections.The role of tracheostomy: Improves weaning parameters (reduced resistance, improved muscle strength, decreased RSBI). 86-patient study: MIP +4.4, MEP +5.4, VT +33.7mL, RSBI -14.6, resistance -4.9 (p<0.01). Mechanisms: reduced dead space, decreased resistance, improved secretion clearance, reduced sedation, decreased aspiration. Timing mixed: 2024 meta-analysis (21 RCTs) early (<=10d) shortened MV (-2.77d)/ICU LOS (-6.36d) but NOT mortality/pneumonia; 2015 Cochrane lower mortality (RR0.83)/higher day-28 discharge; 2023 non-neuro review NO mortality benefit (RR0.91), likely underpowered - conflicting. Observational 508 patients >=14d: trach lower mortality (aHR0.26)/higher weaning success (aHR2.05). Shorter delay time -> higher weaning success. Structured protocol reduced ventilator days 17->10.6, ICU LOS by 4.3 days.

questions.question: A patient fails an SBT three days after a first attempt; two days later they are extubated but need reintubation within 24 hours. How is this second event classified?
options: A: Extubation failure
B: Simple weaning
C: Difficult weaning but not extubation failure
D: Weaning success, since extubation was achieved
E: A normal, expected part of ventilator liberation
questions.explanation: Extubation failure is defined as the need for reintubation after extubation; the reintubation here meets that definition regardless of the preceding weaning course. It is distinct from, though related to, weaning failure (failing an SBT or needing support within 48-72h).
questions.topic: weaning failure extubation failure definitions reintubation

questions.question: A patient needs 5 SBTs over 10 days before successful extubation. How is this classified by the traditional (Boles) and the WIND systems?
options: A: Simple weaning; WIND Group 1
B: Prolonged weaning; WIND Group 1
C: Prolonged weaning (>3 SBTs, >7 days); WIND Group 3
D: Difficult weaning; WIND Group 2
E: It cannot be classified by either system
questions.explanation: More than 3 SBTs and more than 7 days meets the traditional definition of prolonged weaning; under WIND, weaning unresolved 7 days after the first attempt is Group 3 - both systems converge here despite different criteria.
questions.topic: weaning classification simple difficult prolonged WIND groups

questions.question: In the WEAN SAFE study, what was identified as a potentially modifiable factor independently associated with weaning failure?
options: A: The patient's height on admission, recorded at the time of ICU admission
B: The time of day the SBT was performed relative to the nursing shift pattern
C: The patient's blood group, noted on the admission blood tests
D: Higher sedation levels and delayed initiation of ventilator separation
E: The specific ventilator brand used for the patient's mechanical ventilation
questions.explanation: WEAN SAFE identified higher sedation levels and delayed initiation of the first separation attempt as modifiable factors independently associated with failure - an actionable target, unlike non-modifiable factors such as age.
questions.topic: WEAN SAFE sedation modifiable factor delayed separation

questions.question: Compared with patients extubated successfully on the first attempt, patients who need reintubation after extubation failure have approximately what mortality?
options: A: A rate unaffected by reintubation status
B: 1-2%, much like successful extubation
C: 5-10%, only slightly above successful extubation
D: 30-50%, substantially above the 5-10% seen with success
E: A rate that falls after reintubation owing to closer monitoring
questions.explanation: Reintubation after extubation failure carries a mortality of about 30-50%, well above the 5-10% with successful extubation - reflecting both illness severity and harms of the failed episode.
questions.topic: extubation failure reintubation mortality comparison

questions.question: Regarding risk factors for difficult and prolonged weaning from recent studies:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Surgical patients generally have higher extubation-failure rates than medical patients.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Immunocompromised patients have notably higher rates of weaning failure.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Older age and frailty are strong predictors of weaning failure in the WEAN SAFE study.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Chronic cardiac failure present on admission has been associated with worse weaning outcomes.

questions.explanation: Immunocompromised patients having notably higher rates of weaning failure, and older age and frailty being strong predictors of weaning failure in the WEAN SAFE study, are both accurately described findings. The claim that surgical patients generally have higher extubation-failure rates than medical patients is false and reversed — medical patients actually had extubation-failure rates above 20% while surgical patients were below 10%. The claim that chronic cardiac failure present on admission has been associated with worse weaning outcomes is also false and reversed — chronic cardiac failure on admission was, counterintuitively, associated with better outcomes.
questions.topic: weaning risk factors WEAN SAFE frailty immunocompromised surgical medical chronic heart failure

questions.question: What is the main utility of the rapid shallow breathing index (RSBI), and its key limitation?
options: A: It has no relationship to respiratory rate or tidal volume, being derived from other variables
B: It is calculated only from a completed 30-minute SBT, not from a brief early assessment
C: It is validated only in COPD patients, with no evidence in other groups
D: It reliably confirms who will pass an SBT, so a low value alone justifies extubation
E: A sensitive screen for candidates who might pass an SBT, without confirming they will pass
questions.explanation: RSBI (RR/VT after 2 minutes) is a sensitive early screen identifying candidates who may pass a confirmatory SBT, but a favourable value does not confirm actual SBT success, so a formal SBT is still required.
questions.topic: RSBI screening limitation SBT confirmation

questions.question: The weaning index (WI) improves on RSBI by adding two correction factors. How is it calculated?
options: A: Purely from diaphragmatic ultrasound, without RSBI or any breathing-pattern variable
B: It has no defined relationship to RSBI, being a separate index entirely
C: RSBI plus the patient's age, with no other correction
D: RSBI multiplied by an elasticity index (PIP/MIP) and a ventilation demand index (MV/10)
E: RSBI divided by the respiratory rate, with no additional correction factors
questions.explanation: The weaning index is the product of RSBI, the elasticity index (PIP/MIP) and the ventilation demand index (MV/10), combining breathing pattern, mechanics and demand, with higher predictive ability than RSBI alone.
questions.topic: weaning index EI VDI RSBI calculation

questions.question: What does current evidence suggest about diaphragmatic ultrasound indices in weaning?
options: A: Diaphragm ultrasound has no demonstrated predictive value for the outcome of weaning
B: It has replaced all other weaning predictors in guidelines as the sole recommended test
C: It can only diagnose pneumothorax, not predict weaning outcome in ventilated patients
D: Excursion and thickening fraction predict weaning, best combined with the weaning index
E: A higher thickening fraction predicts weaning failure, rather than weaning success
questions.explanation: A meta-analysis found diaphragmatic excursion and thickening fraction predict successful weaning with satisfactory accuracy, and combining the thickening fraction with the weaning index improves accuracy - supporting a multimodal approach.
questions.topic: diaphragm ultrasound thickening fraction excursion weaning predictor combined

questions.question: At what point does the weaning process formally begin?
options: A: Only once the endotracheal tube is physically removed from the patient's airway
B: Weaning has no defined starting point in the process of ventilator liberation
C: Only after a successful spontaneous breathing trial has been completed and passed
D: When the illness that led to ventilation has at least partially resolved
E: At the moment of intubation, regardless of illness status or the reason for ventilation
questions.explanation: Weaning begins once the illness leading to ventilation has at least partially resolved - the first of four steps, preceding readiness assessment, the SBT, and extubation.
questions.topic: weaning first step illness resolution

questions.question: Why might an SBT using low-level pressure support (up to 7 cmH2O) overestimate readiness for extubation, compared with a T-piece?
options: A: T-piece trials are never used in practice, having been abandoned as unreliable
B: Pressure support always increases the work of breathing compared with a T-piece
C: Pressure support and T-piece produce identical conditions for the patient's breathing
D: Pressure support trials cannot be run for any length of time without harming the patient
E: Pressure support lowers the work of breathing, so it may not reflect the post-extubation load
questions.explanation: Low-level pressure support significantly reduces the work of breathing, so a patient may pass while still unable to tolerate the higher post-extubation workload; a T-piece, with no assistance, most closely simulates post-extubation conditions.
questions.topic: SBT T-piece pressure support work of breathing overestimate readiness

questions.question: What SBT duration is generally adequate for most patients, and when is a longer trial (up to 120 minutes) warranted?
options: A: Duration bears no relationship to patient risk factors of any kind
B: 5 minutes suffices for everyone; longer trials are never needed
C: 30 minutes usually suffices; longer (up to 120) in high-risk patients such as elderly or COPD
D: 120 minutes is the standard duration for every patient, regardless of their risk profile
E: All patients need a minimum 24-hour trial before extubation can be considered
questions.explanation: A 30-minute trial is adequate for most patients, but a longer trial (up to 120 minutes) is warranted in those at higher reintubation risk - the elderly and patients with COPD, heart failure, or neuromuscular disorders.
questions.topic: SBT duration 30 120 minutes high risk elderly COPD heart failure neuromuscular

questions.question: Regarding criteria defining the success of a spontaneous breathing trial:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A respiratory rate below 35/min is part of the success criteria.

statements.label: B
statements.text: An SpO2 above 90% or PaO2 above 60 mmHg on FiO2 below 0.4 is part of the success criteria.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A systolic blood pressure of 80-180 mmHg, or a change under 20% from baseline, is consistent with success.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Any increase in heart rate during the trial automatically defines failure, whatever the starting rate.

questions.explanation: A respiratory rate below 35/min, an SpO2 above 90% or PaO2 above 60 mmHg on FiO2 below 0.4, and a systolic blood pressure of 80-180 mmHg (or a change under 20% from baseline), are all accurately described success criteria for a spontaneous breathing trial. The claim that any increase in heart rate during the trial automatically defines failure, whatever the starting rate, is false — the actual criterion is a heart rate below 140/min or heart-rate variability above 20%, not any increase counted as an automatic failure.
questions.topic: SBT success criteria respiratory rate heart rate SpO2 blood pressure

questions.question: A patient on an SBT develops diaphoresis, nasal flaring, abdominal paradox, a heart-rate rise above 40 bpm, and an SpO2 fall above 5%. How should these findings be interpreted?
options: A: Expected features of successful weaning that reassure the treating team
B: A single isolated finding of no significance for the outcome of the trial
C: Multiple failure criteria occurring together, supporting terminating the trial
D: Signs pointing specifically to a pneumothorax rather than trial failure
E: Mild, transient findings compatible with continuing the trial
questions.explanation: Diaphoresis, nasal flaring, abdominal paradox (a sign of muscle dysfunction), a heart-rate rise above 40 bpm and a significant SpO2 fall are several recognised failure criteria occurring together - a strong indication to terminate the trial, unlike isolated or mild findings.
questions.topic: SBT failure criteria clinical signs diaphoresis abdominal paradox tachycardia SpO2

questions.question: An international study found that, compared with early initial SBTs, late initial SBTs were associated with what?
options: A: Improved long-term neurological outcomes at hospital discharge
B: No difference in any outcome measure that was assessed
C: A shorter duration of ventilation and shorter ICU stay
D: A reduced risk of ventilator-associated pneumonia during the ICU stay
E: Longer mechanical ventilation and more prolonged ICU and hospital stay
questions.explanation: Late initial SBTs were associated with longer ventilation and more prolonged ICU and hospital stays than early SBTs, supporting prompt assessment of readiness rather than delay.
questions.topic: SBT timing delay early late duration ventilation ICU stay

questions.question: All of the following are standard readiness-to-wean criteria EXCEPT:
options: A: Haemodynamic stability, such as no continuous vasopressor infusion
B: Adequate cough and secretion management on endotracheal aspiration
C: A rapid shallow breathing index above 150 after 2 minutes of spontaneous breathing
D: Satisfactory oxygenation, e.g. PaO2/FiO2 above 200 mmHg with PEEP at or below 5 cmH2O
E: An adequate conscious level, with the patient awake or easily roused
questions.explanation: An RSBI BELOW 100 (not above 150) is the readiness threshold - a high RSBI reflects rapid shallow breathing and inadequate reserve, the opposite of readiness. B, C, D and E are all correct readiness criteria.
questions.topic: readiness to wean criteria negative stem RSBI oxygenation haemodynamic consciousness EXCEPT

questions.question: A patient fails their first three SBTs. How is this classified, and what does the pathway offer if more than three SBTs are needed without success?
options: A: Simple weaning, with no defined further action available on the pathway
B: Simple weaning, since extubation may still eventually succeed
C: A scenario that cannot be classified by any recognised weaning system
D: Difficult weaning, unrelated to the number of SBT attempts that were required
E: Prolonged weaning; facilitative NIV, then preventive NIV or re-intubation
questions.explanation: More than three SBTs and beyond seven days is prolonged weaning; the pathway then offers facilitative NIV to support extubation, or afterwards preventive NIV or re-intubation, with persistent failure leading to permanent weaning failure.
questions.topic: weaning process simple difficult prolonged pathway facilitative NIV permanent failure

questions.question: After a failed SBT, what are recognised common causes to consider before a repeat trial?
options: A: A single universal cause of inadequate sedation in all patients
B: No identifiable cause, so no further investigation is needed before a repeat trial
C: Patient anxiety alone, requiring only reassurance and no further evaluation
D: Ventilator malfunction alone, requiring only a circuit change before retrying
E: Incomplete illness resolution, errors in readiness assessment, or a new problem
questions.explanation: The common causes of SBT failure are incomplete resolution of the critical illness, errors in readiness assessment, or an emerging new problem - identifying which applies guides the subsequent workup before a repeat SBT.
questions.topic: SBT failure common causes incomplete resolution readiness new problem

questions.question: Regarding the primary pathophysiological mechanisms of weaning failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: These mechanisms can occur in combination in one patient, rather than always in isolation.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Metabolic and endocrine disease is never a cause and can be excluded from the workup.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Neuromuscular disorders and psychological factors are recognised contributors.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Respiratory pump insufficiency and cardiovascular dysfunction are both recognised primary mechanisms.

questions.explanation: These mechanisms can indeed occur in combination in one patient rather than always in isolation, and neuromuscular disorders and psychological factors, alongside respiratory pump insufficiency and cardiovascular dysfunction, are all recognised contributors to weaning failure. The claim that metabolic and endocrine disease is never a cause and can be excluded from the workup is false — metabolic and endocrine disease is itself among the recognised primary mechanisms, and these mechanisms can occur alone or in combination.
questions.topic: weaning failure pathophysiology respiratory cardiovascular neuromuscular psychological metabolic

questions.question: Weaning failure has many recognised contributors, from inadequate reversal of sedation to critical illness myopathy and increased mechanical loads. What does this breadth imply for the diagnostic approach?
options: A: There is a single dominant cause in almost all patients, so a broad survey is unnecessary
B: Ventilator dyssynchrony is never relevant to weaning failure in any patient
C: The pathophysiology is multifactorial, needing a systematic, comprehensive approach
D: These considerations apply only to paediatric patients, not to adults
E: Only endocrine causes are relevant to the development of weaning failure
questions.explanation: Weaning failure can arise from many overlapping domains - drug effects, depressed drive, increased mechanical loads, critical illness myopathy, cardiac disease, dyssynchrony - underscoring the need for a systematic, comprehensive diagnostic approach rather than assuming one cause.
questions.topic: weaning failure multifactorial diagnostic approach systematic

questions.question: Regarding respiratory neuromuscular capability and workload:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Critical illness neuromyopathy is categorised as increased muscle workload rather than decreased capacity.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Phrenic nerve dysfunction and decreased respiratory centre output both reduce respiratory neuromuscular capacity.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Malnutrition is a recognised contributor to decreased respiratory neuromuscular capacity.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Increased CO2 production and increased dead-space ventilation both raise ventilatory requirements and hence muscle workload.

questions.explanation: Phrenic nerve dysfunction and decreased respiratory centre output both reducing respiratory neuromuscular capacity, malnutrition as a recognised contributor to decreased capacity, and increased CO2 production together with increased dead-space ventilation both raising ventilatory requirements and hence muscle workload, are all accurately described. The claim that critical illness neuromyopathy is categorised as increased muscle workload rather than decreased capacity is false and reversed — critical illness neuromyopathy instead reduces neuromuscular capacity through muscle pump dysfunction; it is not a cause of increased workload.
questions.topic: respiratory neuromuscular capacity workload phrenic nerve malnutrition critical illness neuromyopathy

questions.question: Regarding causes of increased airway resistance and reduced compliance in weaning failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Intrinsic PEEP is categorised as a cause of increased airway resistance rather than reduced compliance.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Elevated abdominal pressure and obesity reduce compliance via the chest wall.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Interstitial lung disease and fibrosis are recognised causes of reduced compliance.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Sputum retention, kinking and a small tube diameter all raise airway resistance via the endotracheal tube.

questions.explanation: Elevated abdominal pressure and obesity reducing compliance via the chest wall, interstitial lung disease and fibrosis as recognised causes of reduced compliance, and sputum retention, kinking and a small tube diameter all raising airway resistance via the endotracheal tube, are all accurately described. The claim that intrinsic PEEP is categorised as a cause of increased airway resistance rather than reduced compliance is false and reversed — intrinsic PEEP is instead categorised under reduced (lung) compliance, not increased airway resistance.
questions.topic: airway resistance compliance tube chest wall lung PEEPi interstitial fibrosis

questions.question: A patient meets extubation criteria but has been intubated over 24 hours and is judged at high risk of post-extubation stridor. What test should be considered, and what does it screen for?
options: A: No specific test exists, so extubation should simply proceed
B: A formal swallow study, unrelated to stridor risk or airway patency
C: A repeat chest radiograph, which directly visualises the larynx
D: A cardiac stress test, unrelated to airway concerns in this situation
E: A cuff leak test, screening for laryngeal oedema that could cause stridor
questions.explanation: A cuff leak test is considered in patients at high risk of post-extubation stridor, since laryngeal injury and upper-airway oedema can raise the mechanical workload and cause stridor and extubation failure.
questions.topic: laryngeal injury cuff leak test post-extubation stridor

questions.question: In COPD, what is the primary factor underlying the respiratory muscle dysfunction that contributes to weaning failure?
options: A: An isolated vitamin D deficiency commonly seen in COPD patients
B: Complete absence of any diaphragmatic electrical activity during breathing
C: Excessive muscle strength causing fatigue from overuse of the diaphragm
D: Geometrical distortion of the respiratory muscles due to dynamic hyperinflation
E: Direct toxic injury to the diaphragm from bronchodilators
questions.explanation: In COPD the primary factor is geometrical distortion of the respiratory muscles (particularly the diaphragm) from dynamic hyperinflation, placing them at a mechanical disadvantage on the length-tension curve.
questions.topic: COPD respiratory muscle dysfunction dynamic hyperinflation geometrical distortion

questions.question: Which population is most classically associated with critical illness neuromyopathy contributing to weaning failure?
options: A: Sepsis, COPD, or corticosteroid or neuromuscular-blocker treatment
B: Patients with well-controlled type 2 diabetes and no other illness
C: Patients who have never received sedation during their ICU admission
D: Patients with an isolated ankle fracture and no critical illness
E: Only patients under 30 with no prior history of any medical illness
questions.explanation: Critical illness neuromyopathy is seen especially in sepsis, COPD, and with corticosteroids and/or neuromuscular blockers - recognised risk factors for this common contributor, alongside ventilator-induced diaphragmatic dysfunction.
questions.topic: critical illness neuromyopathy sepsis COPD corticosteroids neuromuscular blockers

questions.question: The tension time index (TTi) quantifies inspiratory muscle contraction. What pattern and threshold are associated with fatigue and weaning failure?
options: A: TTi rising through the trial in those who fail (unchanged in success); above 0.15 means fatigue
B: TTi falling through the trial in those who fail, with values below 0.05 associated with fatigue
C: TTi is only measurable via a pulmonary artery catheter placed for the trial
D: TTi has no relationship to weaning outcome or muscle fatigue
E: A TTi of exactly 1.0 is the normal value in all patients who are ventilated
questions.explanation: TTi rises through the SBT in patients who go on to fail, while remaining unchanged in those who succeed; values above 0.15 are associated with respiratory muscle fatigue.
questions.topic: tension time index TTi fatigue threshold 0.15 SBT

questions.question: All of the following help prevent respiratory muscle atrophy during ventilation EXCEPT:
options: A: Maintaining some inspiratory effort with spontaneous breathing
B: Avoiding excessive positive expiratory pressure to prevent longitudinal atrophy
C: Using proportional modes of assisted ventilation to preserve muscle effort
D: Avoiding myotoxic drugs where possible during the course of ventilation
E: Deliberately maximising sedation and assist for complete muscle rest throughout
questions.explanation: Deliberately maximising sedation and assist for complete rest promotes disuse atrophy - the opposite of protection; the actual aim is to avoid both excessive and inadequate assistance. B, C, D and E are all recognised preventive measures.
questions.topic: respiratory muscle atrophy prevention negative stem disuse excessive assist EXCEPT

questions.question: Cardiovascular dysfunction as a weaning-failure cause was first described by Lemaire et al. in 1988, in which patients? What proportion of unsuccessful SBTs has heart failure been reported to account for?
options: A: Patients with isolated hepatic failure; about 5% of unsuccessful SBTs
B: Paediatric patients only; about 90% of unsuccessful SBTs
C: Patients with isolated renal failure; under 1% of unsuccessful SBTs
D: Patients with obstructive lung disease; up to 42% of unsuccessful SBTs
E: Patients with no cardiac or pulmonary disease; heart failure was never implicated
questions.explanation: It was first described in 1988 by Lemaire et al. in patients with obstructive lung disease; heart failure has since been reported to account for up to 42% of unsuccessful SBTs in a large medical ICU cohort.
questions.topic: cardiovascular dysfunction Lemaire 1988 obstructive lung disease heart failure

questions.question: Roughly what proportion of SBTs are complicated by weaning-induced pulmonary oedema (WiPO), and what proportion of weaning failures does it account for?
options: A: It has never been documented during SBTs
B: About 90% of SBTs, accounting for nearly all failures
C: About 12% of SBTs, accounting for 36% of weaning failures
D: Under 0.1% of SBTs, and it is clinically irrelevant
E: 100% of all weaning failures, with no other cause contributing
questions.explanation: WiPO occurs in about 12% of SBTs and accounts for 36% of weaning failures - a substantial proportion, underscoring the importance of considering cardiac causes when a patient fails.
questions.topic: weaning induced pulmonary oedema WiPO incidence SBT weaning failure

questions.question: Regarding mechanisms of weaning-induced pulmonary oedema:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Increased work of breathing during weaning is associated with decreased extradiaphragmatic and diaphragm muscle activity.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Moving from mechanical ventilation to spontaneous breathing lowers intrathoracic pressure, raising both venous return and left ventricular afterload.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Increased dyspnoea and adrenergic tone with impaired gas exchange can contribute to myocardial ischaemia during a failing trial.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Right ventricular dilation can increase ventricular interdependence, reducing left ventricular compliance.

questions.explanation: Moving from mechanical ventilation to spontaneous breathing lowering intrathoracic pressure and thereby raising both venous return and left ventricular afterload, increased dyspnoea and adrenergic tone with impaired gas exchange contributing to myocardial ischaemia during a failing trial, and right ventricular dilation increasing ventricular interdependence and reducing left ventricular compliance, are all accurately described mechanisms. The claim that increased work of breathing during weaning is associated with decreased extradiaphragmatic and diaphragm muscle activity is false and reversed — increased work of breathing is instead associated with increased muscle activity and decreased intrapleural pressure, not decreased activity.
questions.topic: weaning induced pulmonary oedema mechanism preload afterload interdependence myocardial ischaemia

questions.question: Which combination of pre-existing conditions places a patient at particularly high risk of acute left ventricular dysfunction and WiPO during weaning?
options: A: Isolated well-controlled hypertension with no other disease
B: Isolated mild asymptomatic anaemia
C: A single past episode of self-limiting gastroenteritis
D: Concomitant pre-existing COPD and left ventricular disease
E: A remote appendectomy with no cardiopulmonary disease
questions.explanation: COPD together with pre-existing left ventricular disease carries a particularly high risk of acute LV dysfunction and WiPO - both are independently associated with WiPO, and their combination compounds the risk.
questions.topic: COPD left ventricular disease combination weaning induced pulmonary oedema

questions.question: Which type of left ventricular dysfunction is increasingly emphasised as a weaning-failure contributor, even when ejection fraction is preserved?
options: A: Mitral valve prolapse, unrelated to ventricular function
B: Aortic stenosis only
C: Left ventricular diastolic dysfunction with preserved ejection fraction
D: Right ventricular systolic dysfunction only, with no left-sided contribution
E: Diastolic dysfunction has no relationship to weaning failure
questions.explanation: LV diastolic dysfunction with preserved ejection fraction is increasingly emphasised - in some cohorts weaning failure was more often attributable to diastolic than systolic dysfunction, so diastolic function matters even when ejection fraction looks normal.
questions.topic: left ventricular diastolic dysfunction preserved ejection fraction weaning failure

questions.question: How has the diagnostic approach to WiPO evolved, and what is now the preferred modality?
options: A: Pulmonary artery occlusion pressure remains the only accepted method, with no role for echocardiography
B: Diagnosis relies solely on arterial blood gases, with no cardiac imaging
C: Historically pulmonary artery occlusion pressure; now echocardiography Doppler for LV filling pressures
D: Chest radiography alone is now sufficient and has replaced other modalities
E: BNP measurement has entirely replaced the need for any imaging in this setting
questions.explanation: Pulmonary artery occlusion pressure was historically used, but practice now favours non-invasive modalities, with echocardiography Doppler preferred for detecting elevated LV filling pressures; BNP and other markers are supportive.
questions.topic: weaning induced pulmonary oedema diagnosis echocardiography pulmonary artery occlusion pressure

questions.question: For patients at risk of WiPO, which SBT technique is generally preferred, and why might a T-piece trial still be useful?
options: A: Pressure support is preferred; a T-piece can unmask WiPO susceptibility
B: A T-piece is preferred because it imposes less stress than pressure support
C: SBTs should never be attempted in patients with cardiac risk factors
D: High-frequency oscillatory ventilation is the preferred SBT technique here
E: Neither technique relates to cardiopulmonary stress or WiPO in any way
questions.explanation: Pressure support is generally preferred since a T-piece imposes greater cardiopulmonary stress; but precisely because of that greater stress, a T-piece can be useful in selected patients to unmask susceptibility to WiPO.
questions.topic: SBT technique pressure support T-piece cardiopulmonary stress WiPO unmask

questions.question: Brain dysfunction is a recognised weaning-failure cause, most commonly due to which condition, assessed how?
options: A: Delirium assessed solely by patient self-report, with no validated bedside tool
B: Only structural lesions on CT are relevant, with no role for functional tools
C: Brain dysfunction has no recognised association with weaning
D: Dementia only, assessed by formal neuropsychological testing over weeks
E: Delirium, assessed using the Confusion Assessment Method for the ICU (CAM-ICU)
questions.explanation: Delirium is the main brain-dysfunction cause and is assessed with the CAM-ICU; delirium is associated with difficult weaning and failed extubation, alongside anxiety and depression.
questions.topic: delirium brain dysfunction CAM-ICU weaning failure

questions.question: Regarding the approach to respiratory pump insufficiency as a weaning-failure cause:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Early mobilisation and minimising patient-ventilator asynchrony are recognised therapies for impaired neuromuscular capability.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Tracheotomy is the first-line, most commonly used therapy for increased muscle workload.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Inspecting flow and airway pressure waveforms and measuring dynamic compliance, intrinsic PEEP and resistance are part of the diagnostic approach to increased muscle workload.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Bedside ultrasound of diaphragm descent or thickening, and bilateral phrenic nerve stimulation, assess impaired neuromuscular capability.

questions.explanation: Early mobilisation and minimising patient-ventilator asynchrony as recognised therapies for impaired neuromuscular capability, inspecting flow and airway pressure waveforms with measurement of dynamic compliance, intrinsic PEEP and resistance as part of the diagnostic approach to increased muscle workload, and bedside ultrasound of diaphragm descent or thickening alongside bilateral phrenic nerve stimulation to assess impaired neuromuscular capability, are all accurately described. The claim that tracheotomy is the first-line, most commonly used therapy for increased muscle workload is false — tracheotomy is a therapeutic option but is explicitly used rarely, not first-line; treating the underlying disease and optimising the airway are the primary approaches.
questions.section: Diagnostic workup and management of weaning failure
questions.topic: respiratory pump insufficiency diagnostic therapeutic tracheotomy rarely bronchodilation

questions.question: Regarding the approach to cardiac dysfunction as a weaning-failure cause:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A 12-lead ECG and echocardiography before and during/after the SBT are part of the diagnostic approach.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Monitoring mixed venous oxygen saturation during the SBT is part of the recommended approach.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Diuretics and restrictive fluid management are strategies aimed at reducing afterload, not at optimising fluid balance.

statements.label: D
statements.text: If ischaemia is suspected, treatment may include beta-blockers, anti-platelet therapy, haemoglobin optimisation and percutaneous coronary intervention.

questions.explanation: A 12-lead ECG and echocardiography before and during/after the spontaneous breathing trial, and monitoring mixed venous oxygen saturation during the trial, are both part of the recommended diagnostic approach to cardiac dysfunction as a weaning-failure cause — as is, if ischaemia is suspected, treatment with beta-blockers, anti-platelet therapy, haemoglobin optimisation and percutaneous coronary intervention. The claim that diuretics and restrictive fluid management are strategies aimed at reducing afterload, not at optimising fluid balance, is false and reversed — diuretics and restrictive fluids actually optimise fluid balance; afterload is instead reduced with inotropes/vasodilators such as dobutamine, levosimendan and nitrates.
questions.section: Diagnostic workup and management of weaning failure
questions.topic: cardiac dysfunction diagnostic therapeutic fluid balance afterload myocardial ischaemia

questions.question: For suspected delirium contributing to weaning failure, which is a recognised therapy, and what tests suit suspected metabolic/endocrine causes?
options: A: High-dose benzodiazepines first-line for delirium; no laboratory testing for metabolic causes
B: Physical restraints only for delirium; metabolic causes not identifiable by blood testing
C: Sleep and environment measures have no role in delirium management in the ICU
D: Dexmedetomidine for delirium; electrolytes, glucose and hormone levels for metabolic causes
E: Never treating delirium pharmacologically; diagnosing metabolic causes by examination alone
questions.explanation: Dexmedetomidine, with sedation optimisation, mobilisation and sleep measures, is a recognised delirium therapy; suspected metabolic/endocrine causes are investigated by measuring electrolytes, glucose and relevant hormone levels.
questions.section: Diagnostic workup and management of weaning failure
questions.topic: delirium dexmedetomidine metabolic endocrine electrolytes glucose hormone

questions.question: NIV and HFNC are used at three distinct time points during weaning. What are these three roles?
options: A: Facilitation after failed SBT; prevention if high-risk; curative only in surgical patients
B: Identically at all three time points, with equivalent evidence for each role
C: Only as a facilitation technique, with no preventive or curative use at all
D: Only to treat established post-extubation respiratory failure, with no other role
E: Exclusively in patients with normal lungs and no risk factors for respiratory failure
questions.explanation: NIV/HFNC are used as facilitation after a failed SBT (especially COPD with PaCO2>45), as prevention after a successful SBT in high-risk patients, and as a curative/rescue technique for established failure - where evidence shows no benefit except in surgical patients.
questions.section: The role of NIV and HFNC in weaning failure
questions.topic: NIV HFNC weaning three timepoints facilitation prevention curative

questions.question: All of the following have been associated with NIV as a facilitation technique in hypercapnic COPD patients who fail an SBT EXCEPT:
options: A: Earlier extubation than would otherwise be achieved
B: An increased overall reintubation rate
C: Reduced duration of invasive mechanical ventilation
D: Decreased ICU length of stay
E: A lower incidence of nosocomial pneumonia
questions.explanation: Facilitative NIV in hypercapnic COPD was associated with reduced invasive ventilation, shorter ICU stay and less nosocomial pneumonia - not an increased reintubation rate, which would contradict the rationale.
questions.section: The role of NIV and HFNC in weaning failure
questions.topic: facilitative NIV COPD hypercapnia negative stem reintubation rate EXCEPT

questions.question: Early NIV or HFNC immediately after extubation in patients at high risk of extubation failure (e.g. over 65 with chronic cardiovascular or respiratory disease) has been shown to do what?
options: A: Increase both reintubation and ICU mortality in these high-risk patients
B: Have no effect on reintubation in any group of ventilated patients
C: Benefit only patients under 40 with no comorbidities of any kind
D: Be contraindicated in this preventive setting after extubation
E: Decrease reintubation and ICU mortality, especially in high-risk patients
questions.explanation: Early preventive NIV or HFNC in high-risk patients decreases reintubation and ICU mortality, with a network meta-analysis finding the benefit more pronounced in those at high risk - distinct from the lack of benefit (and possible harm) with rescue NIV.
questions.section: The role of NIV and HFNC in weaning failure
questions.topic: preventive NIV HFNC high risk extubation failure reintubation mortality

questions.question: What has recent evidence shown about alternating NIV with HFNC (versus HFNC alone) after extubation in high-risk patients?
options: A: It significantly increases reintubation compared with HFNC alone
B: It significantly decreases reintubation versus high-flow nasal oxygen alone
C: There is no difference between the strategies in any measured outcome
D: This combined strategy has never been studied in a clinical trial
E: HFNC alone is always contraindicated in high-risk patients after extubation
questions.explanation: Alternating NIV with HFNC, compared with high-flow nasal oxygen alone, significantly decreases reintubation in patients at high risk of extubation failure, potentially combining benefits of both modalities.
questions.section: The role of NIV and HFNC in weaning failure
questions.topic: NIV HFNC alternating reintubation high-flow nasal oxygen

questions.question: Regarding NIV and HFNC as rescue/curative therapy for established post-extubation respiratory failure:

statements.label: A
statements.text: For hypoxaemic post-extubation failure on NIV, intubation is advised if there is no improvement within 1-2 hours.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Two large randomised studies found NIV rescue therapy failed to help and was associated with increased ICU mortality.

statements.label: C
statements.text: One proposed explanation is that preserved oxygenation delays reintubation despite ongoing high work of breathing and self-inflicted lung injury risk.

statements.label: D
statements.text: HFNC has been shown clearly superior to conventional oxygen in reducing treatment failure in this rescue setting.

questions.explanation: For hypoxaemic post-extubation failure on NIV, intubation being advised if there is no improvement within 1-2 hours, two large randomised studies finding NIV rescue therapy failed to help and was associated with increased ICU mortality, and preserved oxygenation being proposed to delay reintubation despite ongoing high work of breathing and self-inflicted lung injury risk, are all accurately described. The claim that HFNC has been shown clearly superior to conventional oxygen in reducing treatment failure in this rescue setting is false and reversed — HFNC was instead shown inferior to conventional oxygen in reducing treatment/reintubation failure in this rescue context, distinct from its more favourable role in the preventive setting.
questions.section: The role of NIV and HFNC in weaning failure
questions.topic: rescue curative NIV post-extubation increased mortality self-inflicted lung injury HFNC inferior

questions.question: A study of 86 difficult-to-wean patients found which change in weaning parameters after tracheostomy?
options: A: Only maximum expiratory pressure changed, with all others static
B: Decreased maximum inspiratory pressure and increased airway resistance
C: Higher inspiratory and expiratory pressures, lower RSBI and resistance
D: No measurable change in any parameter of weaning or respiratory mechanics
E: A marked increase in the rapid shallow breathing index after the procedure
questions.explanation: The study found improvements across parameters after tracheostomy - increased inspiratory/expiratory pressures and tidal volume, decreased RSBI and airway resistance (all significant) - with greater gains in successful weaners.
questions.topic: tracheostomy weaning parameters MIP RSBI resistance

questions.question: Through which mechanisms does tracheostomy facilitate weaning compared with a translaryngeal tube?
options: A: Increased sedation requirements compared with intubation throughout weaning
B: There is no plausible mechanism; any benefit is coincidental
C: Reduced dead space and resistance, better secretion clearance, less sedation
D: Increased dead space and increased airway resistance after the procedure
E: It works solely by directly strengthening the diaphragm muscle fibres
questions.explanation: Proposed mechanisms include reduced dead space, decreased resistance, improved secretion clearance, reduced sedation and lower aspiration risk - though some benefit may also reflect changed clinician behaviour.
questions.topic: tracheostomy mechanisms dead space resistance secretion clearance sedation aspiration

questions.question: Regarding evidence on the timing of tracheostomy:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The 2024 meta-analysis also found early tracheostomy significantly reduced mortality and pneumonia.

statements.label: B
statements.text: A 2015 Cochrane review found lower mortality and a higher probability of ICU discharge by day 28 with early tracheostomy.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A 2024 meta-analysis of 21 RCTs found early tracheostomy (within 10 days) shortened ventilation duration and ICU stay versus late.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A 2023 review in non-neurologically injured patients found no clear mortality benefit and was likely underpowered.

questions.explanation: A 2015 Cochrane review finding lower mortality and a higher probability of ICU discharge by day 28 with early tracheostomy, a 2024 meta-analysis of 21 RCTs finding early tracheostomy (within 10 days) shortened ventilation duration and ICU stay versus late, and a 2023 review in non-neurologically injured patients finding no clear mortality benefit and likely being underpowered, are all accurately described. The claim that the 2024 meta-analysis also found early tracheostomy significantly reduced mortality and pneumonia is false — despite shortening ventilation and ICU stay, the 2024 meta-analysis did not show a significant reduction in mortality or pneumonia; the evidence on mortality remains genuinely conflicting.
questions.topic: tracheostomy timing conflicting 2024 meta-analysis 2015 Cochrane 2023 review mortality

questions.question: A retrospective study of 508 patients ventilated for 14 days or more found tracheostomy (versus continued translaryngeal intubation) was independently associated with what?
options: A: No difference in mortality or weaning success
B: Lower weaning success but no effect on mortality
C: Higher in-hospital mortality and lower weaning success
D: Higher mortality but improved weaning success
E: Lower in-hospital mortality and higher successful weaning rates
questions.explanation: In this study of prolonged ventilation, tracheostomy was independently associated with substantially lower in-hospital mortality (adjusted HR 0.26) and higher weaning success (adjusted HR 2.05) versus continued intubation.
questions.topic: tracheostomy prolonged intubation observational mortality weaning success hazard ratio

questions.question: What has been shown about the time from the decision to perform tracheostomy to the procedure, and about structured weaning protocols?
options: A: Delay time bears no relationship to outcomes, and protocols have never reduced ventilator days
B: Weaning protocols after tracheostomy increase ICU length of stay rather than shorten it
C: A shorter decision-to-procedure delay raises weaning success; protocols reduce ventilator days
D: A longer delay improves weaning success by allowing more recovery time
E: Delay time matters only in patients under 40 years of age
questions.explanation: A shorter decision-to-procedure delay is associated with higher weaning success independent of when the decision was made; a quality-improvement protocol reduced median ventilator days from 17 to 10.6 and ICU stay by 4.3 days.
questions.topic: tracheostomy delay time weaning protocol quality improvement ventilator days
